title: Death of Alexander the Great and dissolution of his empire
summary: On 10 or 11 June 323 BCE Alexander III of Macedon, having in twelve years conquered the Persian empire from the Aegean to the Indus, died of fever in the palace of Nebuchadnezzar II at Babylon at the age of thirty-two. Within days his generals divided the empire among themselves at the Partition of Babylon; within forty years four Hellenistic kingdoms had crystallised out of the wars of the Diadochi. The single Macedonian world Alexander had briefly created never returned, but the Greek-speaking cities he had founded from the Nile to the Oxus reshaped the ancient world for centuries.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Alexander was born at Pella in Macedon in July 356 BCE, the son of King Philip II and his fourth wife Olympias, an Epirote princess. His childhood tutor was Aristotle, sent from the Academy in Athens at Philip's invitation, who taught him at Mieza between 343 and 340 BCE. At sixteen he was regent of Macedon; at eighteen he commanded the left wing at the battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE, which broke the resistance of Thebes and Athens and delivered mainland Greece to Macedonian hegemony. When Philip was assassinated in the theatre at Aegae in 336 BCE, Alexander, then twenty, secured the throne by rapid action and by the murder of possible rivals.

The invasion of Persia, planned by Philip and formally authorised by the League of Corinth as a war of Greek revenge for the invasions of 490 and 480 BCE, began in the spring of 334 BCE. Alexander crossed the Hellespont with about forty thousand infantry and five thousand cavalry, defeated the Persian satraps at the river Granicus in May 334, took Sardis and Miletus, and untangled the Gordian knot at Gordion. In November 333 BCE at Issus in Cilicia he defeated Darius III himself, capturing the Persian king's mother, wife and daughters. A seven-month siege of Tyre in 332 BCE, the taking of Gaza, and the founding of Alexandria at the western edge of the Nile Delta followed. At Gaugamela in October 331 BCE, on a broad plain north of modern Erbil, he defeated Darius a second and decisive time.

Babylon opened its gates. Susa surrendered its treasures. Persepolis was taken and, whether by accident or design, its palace burned in the spring of 330 BCE. Darius, in flight through the Iranian plateau, was murdered by his own satrap Bessus; Alexander pursued and executed the murderer in the following year. From 329 to 327 BCE he campaigned in the far north-east, in Bactria and Sogdiana around the Oxus and Jaxartes, marrying the Bactrian princess Roxana at Balkh. In 327 BCE he crossed the Hindu Kush and descended into the Indus valley. At the Hydaspes in the summer of 326 BCE he defeated the Indian king Porus in one of his hardest battles; at the Hyphasis (the modern Beas), his army finally refused to march further east into the Ganges plain. He turned back down the Indus and marched his army through the harsh Gedrosian desert, losing many of his troops, before regrouping in Persia in 324 BCE.

That winter, at Susa, he presided over a mass marriage of eighty of his Macedonian officers to Persian noblewomen, taking two further wives for himself, and enrolled thirty thousand young Iranians trained in Macedonian arms into his army as the Epigoni. When his veteran troops mutinied at Opis on the Tigris in protest at his integration policy, he broke the mutiny by threatening to demobilise them entirely and to raise a new army from Asian recruits. In the summer of 324 BCE, at Ecbatana, his closest friend and companion Hephaestion died of a fever. Alexander was already, by his officers' accounts, drinking heavily and given to extreme moods. In the late spring of 323 BCE he returned to Babylon, planning an expedition down the Arabian coast.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The chief eyewitness account, the Royal Ephemerides or court diary, is preserved in fragments by Plutarch and Arrian and gives a day-by-day narrative of Alexander's last fortnight. On the evening of 29 May 323 BCE, according to a reconstruction based on the diary, he attended a party thrown by his Thessalian companion Medius of Larissa. The next morning he woke feverish. He continued for several days to give orders, to hold minor commands, and to sacrifice, but the fever did not abate. He was moved from the summer palace on the Euphrates to the older palace of Nebuchadnezzar II inside the city; on 5 or 6 June the fever worsened and he ceased to speak. On the 8th and 9th his troops filed past his bed one by one, in silence; he could only nod or move his eyes. He died on the evening of 10 or 11 June 323 BCE, in the thirty-third year of his life and the thirteenth of his reign. Asked, as tradition has it, on his deathbed to whom he left his kingdom, he answered, To the strongest, kratistôi, and handed his signet ring to his second-in-command, Perdiccas.

The cause of death has been argued ever since. Ancient sources hint at poison, and later Roman romances made much of the theory, naming Iolaus, the son of Alexander's regent Antipater, as the alleged agent. Modern medical historians have canvassed malaria (endemic in the Babylonian marshes), typhoid, West Nile fever, acute pancreatitis brought on by heavy drinking, and, more speculatively, Guillain-Barré syndrome. No diagnosis is certain, and the balance of scholarly opinion is that Alexander died of a natural fever complicated by his own physical exhaustion after a decade of campaigning and by his intense drinking.

His body did not receive burial in Macedon. The Chaldaean and Egyptian embalmers who had been sent for reportedly found the corpse still fresh many days after death, a detail his admirers took as further evidence of his divinity. For nearly two years the body lay at Babylon while a magnificent gold funeral carriage, described in detail by the historian Diodorus, was built to convey it home. In 321 BCE, however, the satrap of Egypt, Ptolemy son of Lagus, intercepted the cortege on the road and diverted it to Memphis, and eventually to Alexandria, where it lay in a great tomb, the Soma, that became a place of pilgrimage for Roman emperors from Augustus to Caracalla.

At Babylon in the weeks after the death, the Macedonian officers convened the settlement now called the Partition of Babylon. Perdiccas, holding the ring, was named regent for a joint kingship of Alexander's half-brother Philip III Arrhidaeus, a man of limited mental capacity, and of the unborn child of Roxana, who was delivered in August 323 BCE and named Alexander IV. The satrapies were distributed. Ptolemy received Egypt and Libya; Lysimachus, Thrace and the western Black Sea coast; Antipater and his lieutenant Craterus, Macedon and Greece; Antigonus Monophthalmus, most of Anatolia; Eumenes of Cardia, Cappadocia and Paphlagonia; Seleucus, initially the command of the elite Companion Cavalry and, from 320 BCE, the satrapy of Babylonia. In the eastern satrapies most local governors were confirmed in place. On paper the empire was still a single kingdom, ruled from Babylon in the name of the two kings, with the regent in the field. In practice it was already dissolving.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The subsequent forty years, the Wars of the Diadochi (Successors), turned the paper unity of the empire into the reality of separate kingdoms. Perdiccas was murdered in Egypt in 320 BCE during a failed invasion aimed at Ptolemy. At Triparadisus in Syria that same year a second partition redistributed the satrapies, with Antipater becoming regent. Antipater's own death in 319 BCE opened a decade of open warfare, in which Antigonus Monophthalmus and his brilliant son Demetrius Poliorcetes bid for control of the whole empire, and lost at the battle of Ipsus in Phrygia in 301 BCE, where Antigonus was killed.

The two kings themselves did not survive. Philip III Arrhidaeus was executed on the orders of Alexander's mother Olympias in 317 BCE; Olympias in turn was killed by Cassander, son of Antipater, in 316 BCE. Alexander IV, kept in nominal kingship, was murdered together with his mother Roxana at Amphipolis in around 309 BCE, on Cassander's orders, when he approached the age at which he might have claimed real power. With him the Argead royal house that had ruled Macedon since the seventh century BCE ended.

By 275 BCE, after a further generation of war and the incursion of Celtic tribes into Anatolia and Greece, four broadly stable Hellenistic kingdoms had emerged. Ptolemaic Egypt, founded by Ptolemy I Soter and ruled from Alexandria, controlled the Nile, Cyprus, Cyrene and often parts of the southern Levant, and would last three centuries until the death of Cleopatra VII in 30 BCE. The Seleucid Empire, founded by Seleucus I Nicator, at its height ran from the Aegean coast of Anatolia to the Indus, though its eastern provinces broke off early: the Mauryan founder Chandragupta pushed Seleucus back to the west of the Hindu Kush in around 305 BCE, and a Greco-Bactrian kingdom later split off. Antigonid Macedon, restored under the descendants of Antigonus Monophthalmus, ruled Macedonia and most of Greece until Roman conquest at Pydna in 168 BCE. In north-western Anatolia the smaller kingdom of Pergamon under the Attalid dynasty flourished from the mid-third century until it was bequeathed to Rome in 133 BCE.

Within those kingdoms, Alexander's most durable legacy was the Greek city. Ancient tradition credited him with founding over seventy poleis; modern scholarship reduces the number of genuine Alexandrias to perhaps twenty, but even the more sober count includes Alexandria in Egypt, Alexandria Eschate on the Jaxartes in Central Asia, Kandahar (Alexandria in Arachosia), Herat (Alexandria in Aria), and a string of garrison towns from Syria to the Indus. Greek became the lingua franca of long-distance trade and administration from Marseilles to the Punjab, and the koine dialect that grew out of Athenian Greek is the language in which the Septuagint translation of the Hebrew Bible was made in third-century Alexandria and in which, three centuries later, the Christian New Testament was composed.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Alexander's personal reputation dominated antiquity as few others have done. Roman generals of the late Republic, Pompey and Julius Caesar among them, consciously imitated his portraits and his gestures; Augustus visited his tomb in Alexandria in 30 BCE, laid a wreath on the coffin, and reportedly broke the corpse's nose by accident. The historians who shaped the tradition, Diodorus, Curtius Rufus, Plutarch, Arrian and Justin, all wrote centuries after his death and drew, directly or through intermediaries, on the lost eyewitness accounts of Ptolemy, Aristobulus and Callisthenes. The result is a portrait at once vivid and elusive: a general of the first rank, a strategist of astonishing daring, and also a man capable of great cruelty (the razing of Thebes in 335 BCE, the massacre at Persepolis, the killing of Cleitus the Black at a drinking party in 328 BCE) and of profound personal grief.

In the medieval and early modern world he was a universal figure. The Alexander Romance, composed in Greek in the third century CE and translated into more than eighty languages, made him the hero of Ethiopian, Armenian, Persian, Arabic, Turkish, Malay and Old French epic. The Persian poet Nizami's Iskandarnama and the appearance of Dhu al-Qarnayn in the Qur'an both draw on the same tradition. Renaissance humanists studied Plutarch's Life; nineteenth-century imperialists used him as a template; twentieth-century scholars have variously admired him as a proto-cosmopolitan idealist and condemned him as a warlord.

Materially, his legacy is everywhere in the museum record of the Hellenistic and Roman worlds. The Alexander Mosaic from the House of the Faun in Pompeii, a first-century BCE copy of a lost Greek painting, shows him leading his Companion Cavalry against Darius at Issus; it is now in the National Archaeological Museum of Naples. His tetradrachm coinage, issued in his lifetime and continued in his name by Ptolemy and Lysimachus, was the standard silver of the Mediterranean for two centuries. And the Greek-lettered inscriptions of the Bactrian kings, the Gandharan Buddhist sculpture that fuses Greek and Indian forms, and the sixty-metre lighthouse Ptolemy raised at Pharos in Alexandria all belong to the world his brief life set in motion.

worldContext.text: In China the Warring States period was in its middle phase. Reforms by Shang Yang in the state of Qin, in force since 356 BCE, had reshaped it into a disciplined, meritocratic, agrarian war-machine that would in the following century absorb all the other states. Mencius (Mengzi), the great Confucian thinker, was travelling between courts. Zhuangzi was composing the Daoist parables that bear his name. The state of Chu, in the middle and lower Yangtze, was the largest of the contending states in area but its coherence was already showing strain.

In Mesoamerica the late Preclassic period was underway. The Zapotec capital at Monte Alban in the Valley of Oaxaca continued to grow, its earliest carved danzante reliefs supplemented by conquest slabs listing subjugated towns. In the Maya lowlands the huge site of El Mirador in the Peten was raising the earliest of its enormous triadic pyramid groups. In the Andes the Chavín cult centres had faded and been replaced by regional cultures such as the Paracas on the south Peruvian coast and the early Nazca in the same region.

In the middle Nile the Meroitic kingdom of Kush was consolidating around its new capital of Meroe, where royal pyramids continued to be raised and the earliest documents in the local Meroitic script were about to appear. Along the North African coast Carthage was engaged in a long, on-and-off war with the Greek cities of Sicily, and had by now planted trading posts on the Atlantic coasts of Morocco and Spain. The Numidian and Berber kingdoms of the interior were absorbing Punic material culture through these contacts.

In the western Pacific the descendants of the Lapita voyagers had by now consolidated ancestral Polynesian societies in Tonga, Samoa and Fiji. Complex horticultural societies, based on breadfruit, taro, coconut and pigs, were sustaining growing populations. The great long-distance voyages of settlement into eastern Polynesia, the Society and Marquesas Islands and beyond, still lay several centuries in the future, but the double-hulled canoe technology that would carry them was maturing.

figures.name: Alexander III the Great
figures.role: King of Macedon 336 to 323 BCE, hegemon of the League of Corinth, conqueror of the Persian empire, and pharaoh of Egypt from 332 BCE

figures.name: Perdiccas
figures.role: Alexander's second-in-command, entrusted with the royal signet ring and named regent at the Partition of Babylon; killed on campaign in Egypt in 320 BCE

figures.name: Ptolemy I Soter
figures.role: Companion of Alexander, satrap and from 305 BCE king of Egypt, founder of the Ptolemaic dynasty that ruled from Alexandria for three centuries

figures.name: Seleucus I Nicator
figures.role: Companion of Alexander, later king of the Seleucid Empire that at its height stretched from the Aegean to the Indus

figures.name: Antigonus I Monophthalmus
figures.role: One-eyed veteran of Philip's and Alexander's wars, satrap of Phrygia, who tried and failed to reunite the empire and was killed at Ipsus in 301 BCE

images.caption: Alexander at the Battle of Issus, detail of the Alexander Mosaic from the House of the Faun in Pompeii, c. 100 BCE, a Roman copy of a lost fourth-century BCE Greek painting. National Archaeological Museum of Naples.
images.credit: Photograph by Ruthven, via Wikimedia Commons; underlying mosaic anonymous

images.caption: Detail of the same Alexander Mosaic, showing the head of Alexander with his characteristic mane of hair. Pompeii, House of the Faun.
images.credit: Unknown ancient artist, via Wikimedia Commons
